risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomization list was prepared using the “sample” function of the S-PLUS® statistical software (Insightful Corporation, Seattle, WA)." Comment: This indicates the allocation sequence was generated by a computer/software randomization function, which meets the criterion for a truly random sequence.

Quote: "Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization." Comment: This explicitly describes sequentially numbered, sealed envelopes used to conceal upcoming assignments from staff enrolling participants, which is an adequate allocation concealment mechanism preventing foreknowledge.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Potential participants were told that the study involved randomization to COCT or to one of two different versions of SMT. At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were not blinded to the broad intervention types (self-management vs continuous oral contraceptive therapy), and the interventions were clearly different and active. Given the subjective outcomes assessed (pain intensity, activity interference, depression, treatment helpfulness), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence reporting and delivery of treatment, so performance bias is high.

Summary: Outcomes were assessed primarily by participant self-report questionnaires/ratings, including the Graded Chronic Pain Scale (characteristic pain intensity and activity interference), the Short Form McGill Pain Questionnaire, the Beck Depression Inventory, and treatment satisfaction/helpfulness ratings. The paper does not report that outcome assessors were blinded, and the participant was effectively the assessor for these subjective outcomes. Comment: Because knowledge of treatment allocation could influence self-reported pain, interference, depression, and satisfaction, detection bias is likely and there is no evidence of blinded outcome assessment.

Quote: "In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment. In the analysis sample, the three treatment groups did not differ significantly in rates of completion of both follow-up assessments (92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT; P = 0.47)... We compared the analysis sample (n = 147) to study participants excluded from analysis (i.e., no follow-up data; n = 24)... There were no significant (P < 0.05) differences in treatment group assignment... or baseline GCPS..." Comment: Attrition was modest and broadly balanced across groups, with no evidence that missingness was related to baseline prognostic factors or differed materially by arm. The paper also reports ITT analyses and multiple imputation sensitivity analyses with unchanged conclusions, which further reduces concern that incomplete outcome data biased the results.
